_meta.authoringNotes: Carapeto FJ et al. Lancet Respir Med 2022 on Group A Strep epidemiology. Tanz RR et al. JAMA Pediatrics on scarlet fever presentations. Gerber MA et al. Clin Infect Dis 2009 IDSA strep pharyngitis guidelines. WHO rheumatic fever/rheumatic heart disease 2018 report.

h1Title: Scarlet Fever: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis and Treatment
heroOverview: Scarlet fever (scarlatina) is an acute exanthematous illness caused by Group A beta-hemolytic Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes, GAS) that produces erythrogenic (pyrogenic) exotoxins (types A, B, and C). The toxins act as superantigens, triggering a diffuse erythematous rash with a characteristic sandpaper texture, along with the typical findings of streptococcal pharyngitis — sore throat, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy. The classic 'scarlet' appearance comes from the combination of the rash plus flushed face with circumoral pallor (pale area around the mouth). Historically a feared disease of childhood with high mortality before the antibiotic era, scarlet fever now carries very low mortality (<0.1%) with appropriate treatment — the disease serves as a marker for GAS infections that may progress to acute rheumatic fever (ARF) and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, particularly in settings where these complications remain common (Pacific islands, sub-Saharan Africa, Aboriginal Australia, and under-resourced populations). Global incidence is estimated at 1-2 per 1,000 children annually in temperate climates; winter-spring seasonal peaks occur. The resurgence of invasive GAS disease with emm1.1 clones producing severe scarlet fever has been noted in China, UK, and Ireland since 2018, with increased reports of severe disease, necrotizing fasciitis, and toxic shock. Diagnosis is clinical with laboratory confirmation by rapid antigen detection test (RADT) or throat culture. Treatment is straightforward: penicillin V oral or benzathine penicillin G IM — resistance to penicillin has never been documented in GAS.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 1-2 per 1,000 children aged 5-15 annually in temperate climates; peak in winter-spring
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects children 5-15 years; uncommon under 3 and over 30; equal sex distribution
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence 6-10 years; mean age of scarlet fever cases 7 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 10-20 million cases of GAS pharyngitis annually in children globally; scarlet fever represents a fraction of GAS infections; acute rheumatic fever affects ~15 million people worldwide with 300,000 deaths/year

definition: Scarlet fever (ICD-10: A38) is a toxin-mediated disease caused by Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) that produces erythrogenic (streptococcal pyrogenic) exotoxins (SPE-A, SPE-B, SPE-C). These superantigens bypass normal antigen processing, directly cross-linking MHC class II molecules on antigen-presenting cells with T-cell receptors, causing massive cytokine release (IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, TNF-α) that produces the characteristic diffuse erythmatous rash, high fever, and systemic symptoms. Only GAS strains that carry genes for one or more of these pyrogenic exotoxins cause scarlet fever — approximately 10-15% of GAS strains. The rash begins on the trunk and spreads centrifugally, sparing the palms and soles. The characteristic 'sandpaper' feel (exanthem with tiny papules) distinguishes it from other viral exanthems. Classic features include strawberry tongue (first white-coated then red), circumoral pallor, Pastia lines (linear petechiae in skin folds), and flushing of the cheeks. The infection localizes in the pharynx (throat) — the source of toxin production — though some cases present with localized skin infection (impetigo) producing scarlet fever. Scarlet fever and the complications of GAS infection (ARF, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, invasive GAS) are managed by infectious disease specialists, pediatricians, and in the case of ARF, cardiologists.

typesClassification.description: The most common form. GAS pharyngitis with erythrogenic toxin production causes sore throat, exudative tonsillitis, fever, and the characteristic rash. This form carries the highest risk of ARF if untreated.

GAS enters through a surgical wound, burn, or skin trauma, producing pyrogenic exotoxins that cause a localized rash around the wound site plus systemic symptoms. Carries higher risk of invasive GAS disease and toxic shock.

A severe complication of GAS infection where pyrogenic exotoxins cause septic shock with multi-organ failure, hypotension, and mortality rates of 30-50%. Presents as scarlet fever with rapid deterioration, shock, and organ dysfunction.

Re-infection with a different GAS strain capable of producing pyrogenic toxin can cause repeated scarlet fever episodes. Each episode carries the same ARF risk and should be treated as a first episode.

primarySymptoms: Sore throat — often severe, with difficulty swallowing, and visible tonsillar exudate (white patches)
High fever — typically 38.5-40°C, often sudden onset with chills
Diffuse erythematous rash — fine, scarlet-colored, sandpaper texture starting on trunk and spreading; blanches with pressure
Circumoral pallor — pale area around the mouth contrasting with flushed cheeks (classic 'strawberry face')
Pastia lines — linear petechial hemorrhages in skin folds (antecubital fossae, axillae, groin)
Strawberry tongue — tongue initially white-coated with prominent red papillae (white strawberry), then denudes to a red surface (red strawberry)
Skin desquamation — fine peeling of fingers, toes, and sometimes trunk 1-2 weeks after rash onset
Headache and malaise — systemic symptoms of streptococcal toxin release
Cervical lymphadenopathy — tender anterior cervical nodes
Vomiting and abdominal pain — particularly in younger children with streptococcal infection
earlyWarningSigns: Severe throat pain with difficulty swallowing and drooling — may indicate tonsillar abscess (quinsy) requiring urgent ENT assessment
Rapidly spreading erythema around a wound with systemic toxicity — possible necrotizing fasciitis or invasive GAS
High fever not responding to antipyretics with deteriorating mental status — scarlet fever toxin-mediated shock
Joint pain and swelling appearing 2-3 weeks after scarlet fever — possible acute rheumatic fever (Jones criteria)
Dark 'cola-colored' urine 1-3 weeks after scarlet fever — possible post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
emergencySigns: Rapid-onset hypotension and shock — streptococcal toxic shock syndrome with 30-50% mortality
Necrotizing fasciitis (pain out of proportion, bullae, skin discoloration) — requires urgent surgical debridement alongside antibiotics
Severe airway obstruction from tonsillar enlargement — quinsy/peritonsillar abscess
Multi-organ dysfunction (renal failure, meningitis, endocarditis) — from untreated invasive GAS
Severe invasive disease (necrotizing fasciitis, STSS) in a child or elderly patient — immediate ICU admission required

causes.cause: Group A Streptococcus (GAS) producing pyrogenic exotoxins
causes.description: Streptococcus pyogenes strains carrying speA, speB, or speC genes produce erythrogenic toxins that act as superantigens, triggering the diffuse rash and systemic symptoms. Only 10-15% of GAS strains carry toxin genes.
GAS spreads via respiratory droplets from infected individuals or asymptomatic carriers. Close contact (school, household) facilitates transmission. Incubation period 1-4 days.
Pyrogenic exotoxins cross-link MHC-II with T-cell receptors, bypassing antigen-specific activation and causing massive cytokine release (cytokine storm). Produces rash, fever, and systemic toxicity.
In surgical/wound scarlet fever, GAS colonizes damaged skin (surgical sites, burns, varicella lesions) and produces toxin systemically.

riskFactors.description: Peak incidence of GAS pharyngitis and scarlet fever in school-age children. Immature immunity to GAS (lack of type-specific antibodies) explains susceptibility.
GAS spreads rapidly in crowded conditions. School outbreaks are common; secondary attack rates of 20-30% within households.

The risk of ARF is directly related to the duration of untreated GAS pharyngitis. Treatment within 9 days of symptom onset prevents ARF. In populations with high ARF burden, prompt treatment is critical.
GAS pharyngitis peaks in winter and spring in temperate climates. Scarlet fever follows the same seasonal pattern.

riskFactors.factor: Prior GAS infection without type-specific immunity
riskFactors.description: Type-specific antibodies to GAS M proteins develop after infection and provide serotype-specific protection. Re-infection with a different M type can cause a new episode of scarlet fever.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 5-15 years in school settings; peak incidence 7 years
Household contacts of scarlet fever cases (secondary attack rate 20-30%)
Resurgence noted in UK/China 2018-2023 with increased severe disease in children
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing scarlet fever is primarily clinical, based on the combination of sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, and pharyngitis with positive Group A Streptococcus (GAS) detection. Throat swabs for rapid antigen detection testing (RADT) and/or bacterial culture are performed on any patient with clinical features of streptococcal pharyngitis — sore throat, tonsillar exudates, fever, anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. RADT has high specificity (>95%) but variable sensitivity (70–90%), so a negative RADT in children should be confirmed by backup culture. ASO (antistreptolysin O) and anti-DNase B titers confirm recent GAS infection and are used when post-streptococcal sequelae are being evaluated. Early scarlet fever is distinguished from other rash-causing illnesses by the characteristic rash pattern — diffuse erythematous sandpaper texture beginning on the trunk, Pastia lines in skin folds, circumoral pallor, and subsequent desquamation of fingertips and toes. In clinically ambiguous cases, differential diagnosis includes Kawasaki disease (requiring fever for ≥5 days and other criteria), viral exanthems, staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, and drug reactions.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid antigen detection test (RADT) on throat swab
diagnosticTests.purpose: Point-of-care confirmation of GAS pharyngitis. Positive result confirms GAS and justifies antibiotic treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Throat swab (both tonsils). Results in 5-10 minutes. Sensitivity 70-90% (lower in children than adults); specificity >95%. Negative RADT in children should be confirmed with throat culture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for GAS pharyngitis. Used to confirm negative RADT in children and for surveillance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Throat swab on blood agar. Incubation 24-48 hours. Confirms beta-hemolytic Streptococcus (GAS identified by bacitracin sensitivity). Use for negative RADT in children before withholding antibiotics.

diagnosticTests.test: ASO (anti-streptolysin O) and anti-DNase B antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Retrospectively confirm recent GAS infection; useful for diagnosing ARF and PSGN when pharyngitis was untreated or culture was not done.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test. ASO rises 1-4 weeks post-infection; peak at 3-6 weeks. Anti-DNase B rises later (6-8 weeks) and remains elevated longer. Both elevated in ARF and PSGN.

diagnosticTests.purpose: For invasive GAS disease, STSS, and surgical scarlet fever. Confirms bacteremia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets before antibiotics if possible. Positive in 60-80% of invasive GAS cases. Sensitivity drops dramatically after antibiotics.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Epidemiological surveillance of circulating strains. Used during outbreak investigation to track transmission and identify hyperinvasive clones.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: PCR and sequencing of the emm gene from throat or wound swabs. Available at reference labs. Important for tracking resurgence and severe disease clones.

treatmentOverview: Scarlet fever is treated identically to GAS pharyngitis: oral penicillin V 500 mg (children: 250 mg) three times daily for 10 days, or amoxicillin 50 mg per kg (maximum 1 g) once daily for 10 days, are the standard first-line regimens. For penicillin-allergic patients, first-generation cephalosporins (such as cefadroxil or cephalexin) are preferred provided there is no history of anaphylaxis to penicillin. For patients with a history of penicillin anaphylaxis, azithromycin, clarithromycin, or clindamycin for 5 to 7 days are acceptable alternatives. Adherence to the full 10-day course is critical to eradicate GAS from the pharynx and prevent post-streptococcal complications. Early discontinuation is the primary driver of treatment failure, recurrent pharyngitis, and rheumatic fever. Antipyretics (acetaminophen or ibuprofen) manage fever and odynophagia. Soft diet and adequate hydration support recovery. Patients are no longer contagious after 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy, allowing safe return to school or work. Household contacts with symptomatic pharyngitis should also be tested and treated per standard GAS pharyngitis protocols. No isolation beyond standard precautions is required after 24 hours of effective therapy. Clinical improvement typically occurs within 48 to 72 hours of starting antibiotics. A follow-up throat culture is not routinely required if symptoms resolve after completing therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin V (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral treatment for GAS pharyngitis/scarlet fever. Bactericidal against GAS; resistance has never been documented. 10-day course achieves maximum cure and ARF prevention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rate >95% when full 10-day course completed. Microbiological eradication 85-95%. ARF prevention near 100% if started within 9 days.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Children: penicillin V 250 mg (or 125 mg <27 kg) 2-3 times daily. Adults: 500 mg twice daily or 250 mg 4 times daily. 10-day course.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzathine penicillin G (Bicillin L-A, IM single dose)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting penicillin administered IM. Provides 3-4 weeks of bactericidal penicillin levels from a single injection — guarantees compliance and is preferred in high ARF burden settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single dose achieves microbiological cure >95% and highest ARF prevention rates. Preferred where oral compliance is uncertain. Equivalent to 10-day oral penicillin in efficacy.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Children <30 kg: 600,000 U IM. Children ≥30 kg and adults: 1.2 million U IM. Given once. Site: upper outer quadrant of gluteal muscle or mid-lateral thigh in children.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: A penicillin class drug with better palatability and absorption than penicillin V. Equal efficacy against GAS. Often used first-line in children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to penicillin V. Better taste and twice-daily dosing improves compliance. Clinical cure >95%, microbiological eradication ~95%.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: 50 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses (max 1g/day) for 10 days. Often given as 25 mg/kg twice daily.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (for penicillin-allergic patients)
medicalTreatments.description: A macrolide antibiotic active against GAS. Used when penicillin allergy is present (anaphylaxis or immediate hypersensitivity). Resistance rates vary by region (0-30%).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure 90-95% in macrolide-susceptible GAS. Rising macrolide resistance in many regions (up to 30% in some US/European settings) limits reliability. Use only when penicillin is contraindicated.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Children: 12 mg/kg once daily for 5 days (max 500 mg day 1, 250 mg days 2-5). Adults: 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2-5.

surgicalOptions.name: Tonsillectomy for recurrent GAS tonsillitis
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of tonsils for patients with very frequent recurrent GAS pharyngitis (7+ episodes/year or 5+/year for 2 years) is considered after careful evaluation. Not indicated for a single episode of scarlet fever.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Elective surgery under general anesthesia. Reserved for recurrent pharyngitis that meets Paradise criteria. Benefits must be weighed against surgical risks (hemorrhage, anesthesia complications).

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of abscesses
surgicalOptions.description: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) complicating scarlet fever pharyngitis requires urgent ENT drainage alongside antibiotics.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Bedside needle aspiration or formal incision and drainage under local anesthesia. Antibiotics continue. May require tonsillectomy after resolution in recurrent cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis
surgicalOptions.description: Necrotizing fasciitis from invasive GAS requires emergent surgical debridement — multiple sessions often needed. Life-saving.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Wide excision of necrotic tissue. Repeated debridement every 24-48 hours until healthy tissue is reached. Combined with IV antibiotics. Mortality reduced from >80% to ~30-40% with urgent surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Clindamycin (for penicillin-allergic patients)
alternativeTreatments.description: A lincosamide antibiotic with excellent GAS coverage and importantly suppresses bacterial toxin production (via ribosomal inhibition) — particularly useful in STSS.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure 90-95%. Toxin suppression property makes it valuable in severe GAS infections and STSS as adjunctive therapy.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: 20 mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses for 10 days (max 450 mg/dose). In STSS, clindamycin is added to standard therapy specifically for toxin suppression.

alternativeTreatments.name: First-generation cephalosporins (cefalexin)
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy. High GAS coverage. Contraindicated in immediate-type penicillin hypersensitivity.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to penicillin V. Clinical cure >95%. Safe in non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy (rash, but not anaphylaxis).
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Children: 25-50 mg/kg/day in 2-4 divided doses for 10 days. Adults: 500 mg 4 times daily or 1g twice daily.

alternativeTreatments.name: Clavulanate-amoxicillin (for treatment failure)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used when compliance issues or GI absorption problems cause apparent treatment failure. Beta-lactamase stable.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Effective against beta-lactamase-producing GAS (rare but documented). Should not be routine first-line due to broader spectrum.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Same dosing as amoxicillin (50 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses) for 10 days.

whySeeSpecialist: Most scarlet fever cases are managed by pediatricians or family physicians. Specialist referral is needed for: invasive GAS disease or STSS (infectious disease), peritonsillar abscess requiring ENT drainage (ENT), acute rheumatic fever with carditis (cardiologist), necrotizing fasciitis (surgeon + ICU), recurrent scarlet fever requiring tonsillectomy evaluation (ENT), and outbreak investigation (public health/infection control).
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek immediate medical care if a child has sore throat with fever and develops a diffuse red rash. A pediatrician or family physician can confirm diagnosis with RADT and initiate treatment. For any signs of severe disease (deteriorating consciousness, hypotension, rapidly spreading wound erythema), go to an emergency department — these may indicate STSS or necrotizing fasciitis requiring ICU care.
hospitalCriteria: Pediatric emergency department for suspected scarlet fever in young infants or with severe systemic symptoms
ENT surgery for peritonsillar abscess drainage and tonsillectomy evaluation
ICU capability for STSS and necrotizing fasciitis management
Microbiology with RADT, throat culture, and GAS emm typing capability
Infection control for hospital outbreak management
keyFacilities: Pediatric inpatient unit for children requiring hospitalization for severe pharyngitis or complications
ENT operating room for abscess drainage and tonsillectomy
ICU for STSS and necrotizing fasciitis care
Cardiology with echocardiography for ARF carditis evaluation
Public health department for GAS outbreak investigation and school surveillance

costBreakdown.consultation: Pediatrician/family physician consultation: ₹200-₹1,000. Emergency department for severe presentations: ₹500-₹2,500.
costBreakdown.medications: Penicillin V 10-day course: ₹50-₹200. Benzathine penicillin G IM (Bicillin L-A): ₹100-₹500. Amoxicillin suspension: ₹80-₹300. Azithromycin: ₹100-₹400.
costBreakdown.surgery: Tonsillectomy: ₹15,000-₹40,000 (if indicated for recurrent episodes). Incision and drainage of quinsy: ₹5,000-₹15,000.

insuranceGuide: Scarlet fever and GAS pharyngitis are covered under most health insurance plans. ARF with carditis is a serious complication covered as a chronic cardiac condition. In India, Ayushman Bharat PMJAY covers hospitalization for severe GAS infections including STSS.
financialAssistance: Penicillin preparations for GAS are inexpensive and widely available. Bharat Petroleum and some state health programs provide free antibiotics for school children with sore throat in high-ARF-burden regions.
preventionStrategies: Prompt antibiotic treatment of GAS pharyngitis within 9 days prevents ARF — the primary prevention strategy
Secondary prophylaxis with monthly benzathine penicillin G IM (Bicillin) prevents recurrent GAS infection and ARF progression in patients with documented ARF
Hand hygiene (soap and water) reduces GAS transmission — alcohol-based hand rub is less effective against GAS
Avoid sharing utensils, cups, and towels in households and school settings during outbreaks
School exclusion of children with scarlet fever until 24 hours after antibiotic treatment started and fever resolved
lifestyleModifications: Teach children proper hand washing technique with soap and water — GAS transmits via respiratory droplets and direct contact
Avoid crowded conditions during winter-spring GAS season when possible
Ensure children complete the full 10-day antibiotic course — stopping early increases ARF risk and promotes resistance
Keep children home from school until 24 hours after antibiotics started and fever resolved
Clean and disinfect toys, surfaces, and shared items during household outbreaks

recommended: Soft, bland foods (soup, rice, yogurt) that are easy to swallow with a sore throat
Cool liquids and ice chips — soothe throat pain and maintain hydration
Nutrient-dense foods for recovery — chicken soup has mild anti-inflammatory properties
Citrus-free soft fruits (bananas, melons) that won't irritate pharynx
avoid: Acidic foods and juices (orange, tomato) that sting the inflamed pharynx
Spicy foods and hot beverages that worsen throat discomfort
Hard, crunchy foods (chips, crackers) that scratch inflamed tonsils
Dairy if it increases pharyngeal mucus (individual variation)

exerciseGuidelines: Children with active scarlet fever (fever, systemic symptoms) should rest at home until fever resolves and they feel well enough to return to normal activities. Light activity is fine once fever-free for 24 hours. Return to school after 24 hours of effective antibiotic treatment and fever resolution. No specific exercise prevents GAS infection — hand hygiene and avoiding close contact during illness are the only proven prevention.
dailyManagement: Administer the full antibiotic course — 10 days of penicillin V or amoxicillin, or a single benzathine penicillin G injection. Do NOT stop when the child feels better.
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and throat pain. Do not give aspirin to children (Reye syndrome risk).
Encourage fluid intake — sore throat reduces oral intake; offer cool liquids, ice pops, and soft foods.
Watch for warning signs: worsening fever after initial improvement, difficulty breathing or swallowing, drooling, or signs of dehydration.
Watch for late complications: joint pain/swelling 2-4 weeks later (ARF), or dark urine 1-3 weeks later (PSGN) — report to doctor immediately.
Keep the child home from school until 24 hours after antibiotics started and fever resolved.
Family members with sore throat should see a doctor for RADT testing — asymptomatic carriers can transmit GAS.
Clean shared items (utensils, towels, toys) in the household to reduce transmission.
prognosis: With appropriate antibiotic therapy, scarlet fever resolves completely within 7–10 days and has an excellent prognosis for full recovery. Post-streptococcal sequelae — rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis — are the primary long-term concerns, but their incidence has fallen dramatically in high-income countries with widespread antibiotic access: rheumatic fever now occurs in fewer than 1% of adequately treated GAS infections in developed countries, and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is similarly uncommon. Scarlet fever recurrence is possible with subsequent GAS infections. In developing regions without reliable antibiotic access, the epidemiological pattern of scarlet fever and its sequelae remains closer to historical rates, illustrating how dramatically modern treatment has altered the natural history of this once-feared illness.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 24-48 hours of starting effective antibiotics. Sore throat improves within 3-5 days. The rash fades within 5-7 days. Skin desquamation (peeling) occurs 1-2 weeks after rash onset and may last 2-4 weeks — this is normal and requires only moisturizers. Return to school is allowed 24 hours after antibiotics started and fever resolved. Full activity resumes within 1-2 weeks. Watch for ARF or PSGN for 4 weeks after infection.
complications: Acute rheumatic fever (ARF) — immune-mediated carditis, arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules; ARF can cause permanent rheumatic heart disease (mitral/aortic valve damage)
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) — immune-complex-mediated nephritis presenting with hematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, and edema 1-3 weeks after GAS infection; usually self-limiting
Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) — localized pus collection next to tonsil causing severe dysphagia, drooling, trismus, and airway risk; requires ENT drainage
Streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (STSS) — superantigen-mediated shock with hypotension, multi-organ failure, and 30-50% mortality
Acute otitis media and sinusitis — secondary bacterial infections following GAS pharyngitis

supportResources.name: WHO Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease Programme
supportResources.description: Global strategy for RF/RHD control including secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin G

supportResources.name: CDC Group A Streptococcal Disease Information
supportResources.description: Up-to-date US surveillance data, treatment guidelines, and outbreak reporting for GAS infections

supportResources.name: National Health Mission India - RHD Control Programme
supportResources.description: India's programme for secondary prophylaxis of ARF with monthly Bicillin injections

confusedWithConditions.name: Kawasaki disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fever and rash in children. Kawasaki has prolonged fever (≥5 days), bilateral conjunctivitis, oral changes (strawberry tongue, cracked lips), polymorphous rash, and extremity changes. Scarlet fever has acute onset, prominent pharyngitis, and circumoral pallor. Kawasaki requires echocardiography and IVIG treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Viral exanthem (measles, rubella, roseola)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral exanthems typically lack the pharyngitis of scarlet fever, have different rash distribution and texture (not sandpaper), and follow a different prodrome. Measles has Koplik spots and cough/conjunctivitis; roseola has high fever followed by rash as fever resolves.

confusedWithConditions.name: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SSSS is caused by Staphylococcus aureus exfoliative toxins and presents with diffuse tender erythema followed by widespread skin peeling in infants and young children. There is typically no pharyngitis. Distinguished by absence of GAS pharyngitis and different age group.

coOccurringConditions.name: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
coOccurringConditions.reason: Immune complex deposition in glomeruli 1-3 weeks after GAS infection causes hematuria and hypertension; can follow scarlet fever even after appropriate treatment (treatment prevents ARF but not PSGN)

coOccurringConditions.name: Acute rheumatic fever
coOccurringConditions.reason: Molecular mimicry between GAS M protein and cardiac tissue triggers autoimmune inflammation; prevented by antibiotics given within 9 days — treatment does not prevent PSGN

coOccurringConditions.name: Acute otitis media
coOccurringConditions.reason: Secondary bacterial infection of the middle ear following GAS pharyngitis; common complication in children with poor eustachian tube function

relatedConditions.name: Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis
relatedConditions.relationship: The underlying infection that causes scarlet fever — all scarlet fever cases have GAS pharyngitis (or wound infection as source)

relatedConditions.name: Acute rheumatic fever
relatedConditions.relationship: The primary non-suppurative complication of untreated GAS pharyngitis; can cause permanent heart valve damage

relatedConditions.name: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
relatedConditions.relationship: An immune-complex complication of GAS that can follow appropriately treated scarlet fever — different mechanism from ARF

relatedConditions.name: Impetigo
relatedConditions.relationship: A superficial GAS skin infection; shares epidemiology and treatment approach with pharyngeal GAS; can rarely cause scarlet fever if toxin-producing strain infects skin

faqs.question: What is scarlet fever and what causes it?
faqs.answer: Scarlet fever is a childhood illness caused by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) — the same bacteria that cause strep throat. When certain GAS strains produce pyrogenic (fever-causing) toxins, these toxins spread through the bloodstream and cause the characteristic red rash and other symptoms. The 'scarlet' in the name refers to the bright red color of the rash. It is not caused by a virus — antibiotics can cure it.

faqs.question: What does scarlet fever rash look like?
faqs.answer: The scarlet fever rash is a fine, red, sandpaper-textured rash that starts on the chest and trunk and spreads to the arms and legs. It blanches (turns white) when you press on it. Key signs: flushed cheeks with a pale area around the mouth (circumoral pallor), pink lines in the skin folds (Pastia lines), and later, peeling of the skin on fingers and toes. The face is red but the area around the mouth is white.

faqs.question: How is scarlet fever treated?
faqs.answer: Scarlet fever is treated with antibiotics — penicillin or amoxicillin for 10 days is first-line. A single injection of benzathine penicillin G (a long-acting penicillin) is very effective and guarantees the medicine is taken. For children allergic to penicillin, azithromycin or clindamycin are alternatives. With proper antibiotic treatment started within 9 days of symptoms, scarlet fever is completely curable with essentially zero mortality.

faqs.question: Is scarlet fever contagious? How does it spread?
faqs.answer: Yes, scarlet fever is very contagious. GAS spreads through tiny droplets from the infected person's throat or nose — when they cough, sneeze, or even talk. Close contact (sharing utensils, kissing) or being in the same classroom/household spreads it. Children with scarlet fever should stay home from school until 24 hours after antibiotics started and fever resolved. GAS is not spread through casual contact like doorknobs.

faqs.question: Can scarlet fever come back?
faqs.answer: Yes, scarlet fever can recur if the child is infected with a different GAS strain that produces pyrogenic toxins. Immunity to one GAS strain (based on its M protein type) does not protect against other strains. Recurrent episodes carry the same ARF risk and require the same prompt antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: What is the connection between scarlet fever and rheumatic fever?
faqs.answer: Untreated GAS pharyngitis (including cases that present as scarlet fever) can trigger acute rheumatic fever (ARF) — an immune reaction that causes inflammation of the heart (carditis), joints (arthritis), skin, and nervous system. ARF can permanently damage heart valves (rheumatic heart disease). The key prevention: give antibiotics within 9 days of sore throat/fever starting. Treating scarlet fever with antibiotics prevents ARF.

faqs.question: What is the difference between scarlet fever and rheumatic fever?
faqs.answer: Scarlet fever is the acute bacterial infection itself — treated with antibiotics in 1-2 weeks. Rheumatic fever is an immune complication that can develop 2-4 weeks after untreated GAS infection, affecting the heart, joints, and nervous system. Rheumatic fever can cause permanent heart valve damage and requires long-term penicillin prophylaxis to prevent recurrent GAS infections.

faqs.question: What is 'strawberry tongue' and does it mean scarlet fever?
faqs.answer: Strawberry tongue is a red, bumpy appearance of the tongue seen in scarlet fever (and also in Kawasaki disease). In scarlet fever, the tongue is initially white-coated with prominent red bumps (white strawberry tongue), then the white coating sheds leaving a bright red surface (red strawberry tongue). It is caused by the same pyrogenic toxin that causes the rash and resolves with treatment.

faqs.question: How long is the incubation period for scarlet fever?
faqs.answer: The incubation period — time from exposure to symptoms — is typically 1-4 days for scarlet fever. This means if your child was exposed to someone with scarlet fever at school, symptoms would appear within 1-4 days. During this time, the child may be contagious even before symptoms appear. Close contacts who develop sore throat or fever should see a doctor for testing.

faqs.question: Is scarlet fever dangerous?
faqs.answer: In the modern era with antibiotic treatment, scarlet fever is not dangerous — near-zero mortality with prompt treatment. However, if untreated, it can lead to serious complications: acute rheumatic fever (which can permanently damage heart valves), post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (kidney inflammation causing bloody urine), and rare severe invasive GAS infections (necrotizing fasciitis, toxic shock). These complications are prevented by prompt antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: Why did scarlet fever increase in the UK and China recently?
faqs.answer: Epidemiologists have noted a resurgence of severe GAS infections including scarlet fever in the UK (2018 onward), China, Ireland, and other countries since 2018. The cause appears to be related to specific GAS clones (emm1.1 in the UK) that produce more pyrogenic toxins and cause more invasive disease. Increased testing and reporting also contribute to apparent increases. The rise is being monitored closely by public health authorities.

faqs.question: When can my child return to school after scarlet fever?
faqs.answer: Your child can return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics AND once they are fever-free. This prevents spread to other children. Ensure the full 10-day antibiotic course is completed. Watch for late complications (joint pain, unusual movements, or dark urine) in the 4 weeks following scarlet fever and report these to your doctor immediately.
